stories.title: The Spice-Merchant's Threshold
stories.text: Raen had traded in impossible things for three years before she understood what she was really selling. At the Wailing Scar—that crack in reality where three timelines bled into one market—merchants from all iterations gathered to move goods that should not exist: pre-Ascension starlight in glass vials, ledgers from worlds that no longer were, consciousness-fragments that sang in voices belonging to the dead. Raen's stall sat at the market's edge, where the air shimmered between states, where you could see both the Scar's rain and the sun of a different sky at once. She sold spice. Cardamom and saffron from the Scorched Reach—real spice, grown in soil, tangible. "My daughter," a woman whispered, clutching a fragment no bigger than a tooth. "She's scattered. Dominion auditors took her apart, made her into currency. I have three pieces. The Remaking had shattered consciousness itself. Now it drifted like ash, traded by the desperate, hoarded by wyverns, weaponized by the Dominion's phantom-ledgers. But at the Wailing Scar, the rules bent. Fragments here hummed with something like intention.

stories.title: The Dwarf-Smith's Inheritance
stories.text:
Korvin Ironfoot was dying, and he had one blade left to forge. His forge lay deep beneath the Ironspine Mountains, in caverns where the stone remembered the songs of master-smiths dead three centuries. Korvin had inherited this forge from his grandmother, who had inherited it from hers—an unbroken lineage of weapon-crafters whose names were written not in ledgers but in the metal itself. He was ninety-four. His apprentice, young Merra, stood at the furnace with tears in her eyes. "A warlord from the Shattered West. Wants a spear. Wants it to recognize true nobility—to sing for a rightful heir and refuse the unworthy."

"You've never made such a thing," Merra whispered. "No. But my grandmother told me the method before she died. Said it would be needed when the world broke again." He withdrew the iron—it glowed like a captured star. "The Remaking has shattered bloodlines as surely as it shattered gold. Kings don't know their own heirs now. Ledgers lie.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Last Apprentice
stories.text:
The refugee camp sprawled across wind-scoured plateaus where trade routes had once been. Dalen worked in the cooking tent, boiling roots and hanging fish to dry, keeping the younger children fed. They had no parents—none of the children there did. The Remaking had scattered those bloodlines into fragments that no one could afford to reassemble. When Naela arrived—bone-white hair, fingers stained with carving dust—the bone-singers announced their purpose: teach the Elder Tongue to those who could hear it. The language that predated kingdoms. The language that consciousness-fragments understood. Dalen did not volunteer. But Naela's eyes found them in the crowd, and she spoke a single syllable—a sound like water remembering where it had flowed—and Dalen felt something crack open in their chest. "You," Naela said. "You were made scattered. The fragments still speak to you in your sleep."

It was true. Dalen had never told anyone about the voices. But the fragments did speak—not in words, but in feeling. A mother's laugh. A sibling's anger. A stranger's grief, mistaken for their own.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Winter Gambit
stories.text: Sorrath had hoarded consciousness-fragments for two hundred years. Her cavern beneath the Ironspine held mountains of them—shimmering, singing, each one a piece of someone else's soul, purchased at the price of desperate refugees' survival. Then the Remaking broke the world, and gold became dust, and Sorrath discovered that her hoard was also dust. Not dead—singing, as it always had. But worthless in any market the Dominion recognized. The fragments had become so plentiful that even wyvern-lords found their hoards suddenly, bitterly ordinary. Sorrath had always been the cruelest of her kind. She had negotiated deliberately, purchased at extremes, kept the most beautiful fragments for herself while selling the damaged ones to bone-singers and black-market forgers. But now, sitting atop her useless mountain, she understood: she had traded in nothing but loneliness. A refugee band—perhaps forty souls—became trapped in the foothills near her lair, snow blocking all passes. Sorrath watched them from the peaks, watched them huddle, watched them begin to die. She could have flown away. Instead, she descended.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Gamble
stories.text: The Dominion's vault lay seven floors beneath Keshtir's administrative heart, sealed by locks of meteoric iron and bone-resonance that should have been impossible to pick. Thom Bronzelock had spent forty years learning the Dominion's secrets, studying their mechanisms, waiting for the moment when the empire's authority would fracture enough that one small act of defiance might matter. Thom's daughter—scattered across four consciousness-fragments by an auditor's cruel tally—remained locked in the Quarantine-House, where the Dominion kept refugees deemed too unstable for release. They had promised to restore her if Thom forged a "truth-lock" for them: a mechanism that would identify which refugee testimonies were genuine and which were Counter-Archive lies. Thom had refused. Which meant his daughter remained scattered. But now the Dominion was breaking. Auditors murdered auditors over conflicting ledgers. Regional commanders claimed independence. The empire was eating itself, and in the chaos, Thom saw his chance. He moved through the vault with a thief's grace, his fingers reading locks the way a bone-singer read the Elder Tongue.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Reckoning
stories.text:
Mella had burned her hands twice before she learned the furnace would not forgive carelessness. Now, in the Lampwick Quarter's deepest kiln-house, she faced a choice that made both scars ache. The Dominion auditor stood at the threshold, ledger in hand, demanding the consciousness-fragments she'd been smuggling out in glass vessels. For three months, she'd hidden them—whispers of stolen selves, scattered minds given refuge in crafted forms. The auditor's eyes held the cold certainty of someone who'd already written her arrest. "You cannot hold what the Dominion claims," the auditor said. "Submit the vessels, or the Quarter burns."

Mella looked at the furnace. Its heat remembered. She'd heard the old songs from her grandmother—forge-chants that bent glass and memory together. They were forbidden now, outlawed the moment consciousness became currency. But her hands knew them. The scars on her palms tingled with recognition. She began to sing. The auditor stepped forward, but Mella had already plunged her hands into the molten glass. The pain was exquisite—ancient, pure.

stories.title: The Shepherd's Mercy
stories.text:
Kestran had kept his shepherd's flocks in the High Meadows of the Steppe Marches for seventeen years, asking nothing of the world but wind and grass. The wyvern that landed among his sheep should have killed half the herd. Instead, it wept. Velka was ancient—three centuries of hoarding had worn her golden hide thin, and the Remaking had rendered her entire accumulated fortune into worthless dust. She had flown north to die, but instead found this man with callused hands, sitting unmoved as a dragon descended before him. "They were all I had," Velka rasped, her voice like stone scraping stone. "Every coin, every jewel. I kept them close. And now—nothing."

Kestran studied her with the patience of someone who tended creatures far more fragile than they appeared. He gestured to his flock. "These sheep are not valuable in coin. But I know each by name. I know which ewe bears twins, which lamb was born lame and healed despite it.

stories.title: The Reef-Singer's Mercy
stories.text: The Tear of the Drowned God was failing, and Captain Merith of the Sapphire Deep had three days to find a sacrifice. For a thousand years, the deepkin covenant had been simple: one willing death every seventh generation, blood spilled to renew the seal. But Merith had fractured into seven autonomous echoes when reality shattered, and none of them agreed on what "willing" meant anymore. Some echoes believed the old way was mercy. Others saw it as murder dressed in ritual. She dove to the deepest chamber where the Tear trembled, its light stuttering like a dying heart. There, carved in bone older than kingdoms, was the Seal-Song—the ritual that demanded sacrifice. But Merith had been listening to her crew sing. Humans, deepkin, and threshold-beings working together in the Sapphire Deep's depths, their voices braiding across the water. They did not sing of death. They sang of *choice*—the idea that renewal might come not from a single sacrifice, but from countless small choices made daily, renewed each dawn.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Blind Map
stories.text: Henryk's greatest fear was that his maps were too accurate. He had been the Dominion's master cartographer for twenty years, recording the world with obsessive precision. But after the Remaking, when he discovered that the world had already collapsed eight times before in identical patterns, and that his maps might be the very tool that *triggered* the ninth iteration, Henryk made a choice: he blinded himself. Now, years later, he taught refugees and rebel scribes to navigate by sound—by the memory of birdsong, by the rhythm of footfalls on different terrains, by the way wind spoke through stone and grass. The Counter-Archive had hired him to train their couriers, but his true work was creating false ninth maps. Maps so deliberately incomplete, so riddled with paradox and misdirection, that anyone following them would become hopelessly lost. Maps that did not *describe* the world but *confused* it. Kesla, a young cartographer's apprentice with sharp eyes and sharper questions, asked him the question he'd been dreading: "But what if the incomplete map is itself the trigger?

stories.title: The Locksmith's Liberation
stories.text: Gareth the Locksmith had been imprisoned in Keshtir's deepest vault for three months before he realized his jailers had made a fundamental mistake: they'd locked him in with the locks he'd helped design. The Dominion wanted him to build a master-lock—something that could secure consciousness-fragments so completely that they could never be stolen, traded, or freed. It was the auditor's dream: perfect control, perfect ledger-logic, perfect imprisonment of the scattered shards that had become the world's only currency. Gareth began the work. He was, after all, the finest locksmith in three provinces. But as he carved the binding-mechanism, he did something subversive: he built in seventeen hidden release points, each one keyed to a different emotion. Rage would spring the eastern latch. Sorrow would unlock the northern mechanism. Joy would open the southern gate. And love—true, fierce love—would dismantle the entire working from within. He knew he would die for this. The Dominion did not forgive sabotage.
